label: Pronouns (object, reflexive, ne, ci, combined, position)
description: Italian object pronouns: direct (DOP), indirect (IOP), reflexive, partitive ne, locative ci, combined clitic clusters (me lo, glielo, me ne, gliene), and positioning rules (pre-verb, post-infinitive, post-imperative, etc.).

label: Direct object pronouns (DOPs)
description: Mi, ti, lo, la, ci, vi, li, le. Replace the direct object of a transitive verb. Default position is pre-verb in finite tenses.

label: mi (me, 1sg)
description: First-person singular DOP. 'Marco mi vede.' Same form as IOP and reflexive 1sg.

attributes.common_miss: Marco vede me (using stressed pronoun instead)

label: ti (you-informal, 2sg)
description: Second-person singular informal DOP.

label: lo (him / it, masc 3sg)
description: Third-person masculine singular DOP. Elides to l' before a vowel: 'lo vedo / l'ho visto'.

label: la (her / it, fem 3sg)
description: Third-person feminine singular DOP. Elides to l' before a vowel.

label: ci (us, 1pl), DOP sense
description: First-person plural DOP. Ambiguous in surface form with reflexive and locative ci; context disambiguates.

label: vi (you, 2pl)
description: Second-person plural DOP.

label: li (them, masc 3pl)
description: Third-person masculine plural DOP. No elision before vowel ('li ho visti', not 'l'ho visti').

attributes.common_miss: gli (confusion with IOP) or li' (incorrect elision)

label: le (them, fem 3pl)
description: Third-person feminine plural DOP. No elision before vowel.

label: La (formal Lei, you-formal-sg DOP)
description: Formal address singular DOP. Same surface form as feminine 3sg 'la' but addressed to a single 'Lei' regardless of biological gender of the addressee. Often capitalised in formal writing.

label: Elision: lo/la → l' before a vowel
description: Singular lo and la elide to l' before a verb starting with a vowel: 'l'ho visto'. li and le do NOT elide. The elision is obligatory in standard written Italian.

label: Indirect object pronouns (IOPs)
description: Mi, ti, gli, le, ci, vi, gli (for loro). Replace the indirect object (often translated 'to me / to him / to her'). Position rules match DOPs.

label: mi (to me, 1sg)
description: Identical surface form to DOP mi; the verb's argument structure tells you which is meant.

label: ti (to you-informal, 2sg)
description: Same surface form as DOP ti.

label: gli (to him, masc 3sg)
description: Third-person masculine singular IOP. Not to be confused with the article 'gli' or with DOP 'li'.

label: le (to her, fem 3sg)
description: Third-person feminine singular IOP. Same surface form as DOP fem 3pl 'le'; verb argument tells which.

label: ci (to us, 1pl)
description: First-person plural IOP. Same form as DOP ci, reflexive ci, and locative ci.

label: vi (to you, 2pl)
description: Second-person plural IOP.

label: gli or loro (to them, 3pl)
description: Modern spoken usage prefers gli for both masc and fem plural; formal writing may use 'loro' post-verb ('Ho detto loro la verità'). Both grammatical; gli has won the everyday register.

attributes.canonical_example: Gli ho parlato (modern). Ho parlato loro (formal).

label: Le (formal Lei, to-you-formal-sg)
description: Formal address singular IOP. Same surface form as feminine 3sg IOP. Often capitalised in formal writing.

label: Verbs whose 'subject' surfaces as an IOP (piacere, mancare, etc.)
description: A class of verbs where the English-style subject ('I like') maps to an Italian IOP ('mi piace' = literally 'to-me it-pleases'). Includes piacere, mancare, bastare, sembrare, occorrere, servire. Auxiliary in PP is essere; participle agrees with the grammatical subject (the 'thing liked').

attributes.canonical_example: Mi piace la pizza. / Le sono mancate le sue amiche.

label: Reflexive pronouns
description: Mi, ti, si, ci, vi, si. Used with reflexive verbs (lavarsi, alzarsi, divertirsi). All reflexive verbs take essere as their auxiliary in compound tenses, and the participle agrees with the subject.

label: mi (myself, 1sg)
description: Identical surface form to DOP/IOP mi.

label: si (himself/herself/oneself/yourself-formal, 3sg)
description: Third-person singular reflexive. Same form for masc, fem, and formal Lei.

attributes.canonical_example: Marco si lava. / Lei si chiama Anna.

label: ci (ourselves, 1pl)
description: Same surface form as DOP/IOP ci and locative ci.

label: si (themselves, 3pl)
description: Same form as 3sg reflexive si.

label: Reflexive verbs always take essere in compound tenses
description: Even verbs that are non-reflexively avere-takers switch to essere when reflexive: 'ho lavato la macchina' but 'mi sono lavato'. The participle then agrees with the subject.

attributes.canonical_example: Mi sono lavato/a. Ci siamo divertiti/e.

label: Reflexive plus an object pronoun (me lo lavo)
description: When a reflexive verb has both a subject (whose action reflects) and an object (the thing being done to oneself), the reflexive pronoun combines with the DOP: 'mi lavo le mani' → 'me le lavo' (vowel change mi→me before the DOP).

attributes.canonical_example: Me le lavo. Se l'è lavata.

label: Combined pronouns (clitic clusters)
description: When IOP + DOP appear together, the IOP comes first and the vowel of mi/ti/ci/vi changes from -i to -e. Third-person gli/le become glie- written attached to the DOP (glielo, gliela, glieli, gliele, gliene).

label: Order: IOP before DOP
description: When both an IOP and a DOP precede the verb, the IOP comes first: 'me lo dai', not 'lo mi dai'.

label: Vowel change: mi/ti/ci/vi → me/te/ce/ve before lo/la/li/le/ne
description: The final -i of mi, ti, ci, vi becomes -e when followed by another clitic. 'mi lo dai' is not valid; the form is 'me lo dai'.

attributes.canonical_example: me lo / te lo / ce lo / ve lo

label: gli/le + lo/la/li/le → glielo, gliela, glieli, gliele (written attached)
description: Third-person IOP (gli for masc, le for fem) combines with a DOP and is written as one word: glielo, gliela, glieli, gliele. The feminine IOP le takes the same form as the masculine gli in this combination. Also: gliene (gli/le + ne).

attributes.canonical_example: Glielo dico. (= I tell it to him/her.)
attributes.common_miss: gli lo dico (not attached) or le lo dico (no glie- transform)

label: me ne, te ne, ce ne, ve ne (IOP + partitive ne)
description: When ne combines with an IOP, the same vowel change applies: 'me ne dai tre'.

attributes.canonical_example: Me ne dai tre? Gliene parlo domani.

label: ne (partitive / pronominal)
description: Multi-purpose clitic. Partitive ('how many of them?' → ne ho tre), pronominal of-it/of-them (ne parliamo), and motion 'from there' (me ne vado). Triggers participle agreement in passato prossimo when partitive.

label: Partitive ne (some of X, three of them, etc.)
description: Replaces a noun phrase introduced by 'di' or a partitive amount: 'Quanti libri hai?' 'Ne ho tre.' / 'Hai del pane?' 'Sì, ne ho.'

attributes.canonical_example: Ne ho tre. / Ne voglio un po'.

label: Pronominal ne (of it / about it)
description: Replaces a 'di + noun' or 'di + topic' phrase: 'Parli del libro?' 'Sì, ne parlo.'

attributes.canonical_example: Ne parliamo domani. / Cosa ne pensi?

label: ne with passato prossimo participle agreement
description: Partitive ne triggers agreement on the participle with the implicit antecedent's gender and number. Cross-references verb_form.passato_prossimo.participle_agreement.with_avere.preceding_partitive_ne.

attributes.canonical_example: Hai mangiato le mele? Sì, ne ho mangiate tre.

label: ne in motion expressions (andarsene, venirsene)
description: Pronominal ne in idiomatic 'leaving' or 'going from here': 'me ne vado', 'se ne andò'. Combines reflexive + ne in a fixed pattern.

attributes.canonical_example: Me ne vado. Se ne andarono.

label: ci (locative / 'there' / idiomatic)
description: Locative ci means 'there' or 'about it / in it'. Distinct from DOP/IOP/reflexive ci in function. Also appears in 'c'è / ci sono' existentials and in idiomatic verbs (tenerci, metterci, volerci, farcela, avercela).

label: ci as 'there' (replacing a location)
description: Replaces a location phrase: 'Vai a Roma?' 'Sì, ci vado.'

attributes.canonical_example: Ci vado domani. (= I'm going there tomorrow.)

label: c'è / ci sono (there is / there are)
description: Existential construction. Singular: c'è (elided from ci è). Plural: ci sono.

attributes.canonical_example: C'è un problema. Ci sono molti libri.

label: ci as 'about it / in it' (pronominal of a-noun)
description: Replaces 'a + noun/topic': 'Pensi al lavoro?' 'Sì, ci penso.'

label: Idiomatic verbs with built-in ci (tenerci, metterci, volerci, farcela)
description: A class of verbs where ci is lexicalised: tenerci a (to care about), metterci tempo (to take time), volerci (to need/take), farcela (to manage), avercela con (to have it in for).

attributes.canonical_example: Ci tengo molto. / Ci vogliono due ore. / Non ce la faccio.

label: Distinguishing ci vs ne (a- vs di-)
description: ci replaces a-phrases (a Roma, al lavoro), ne replaces di-phrases (del libro, di questo). Common confusion at B1.

attributes.canonical_example: Penso al lavoro → ci penso. Parlo del libro → ne parlo.

label: Pronoun positioning rules
description: Where a clitic pronoun sits relative to the verb. Default is pre-verb in finite tenses. Attached post-verb on infinitives, gerunds, tu/noi/voi imperatives, and past participles in absolute constructions.

label: Pre-verb (default for finite tenses)
description: In present, imperfect, passato prossimo, future, conditional, subjunctive: the pronoun goes immediately before the (auxiliary or finite) verb. 'Lo vedo. L'ho visto. Lo vedrò.'

label: Attached to an infinitive (vederlo, parlargli)
description: On an infinitive (citation form or in a verb-plus-infinitive construction), the pronoun attaches to the end. The infinitive drops its final -e: vedere + lo → vederlo, parlare + gli → parlargli.

attributes.common_miss: Voglio lo vedere (separated) or vedere lo (separated)

label: With modal + infinitive: either pre-modal or attached to infinitive
description: With dovere, potere, volere, sapere + infinitive, the clitic can either come before the modal or attach to the infinitive: 'Lo voglio vedere' or 'Voglio vederlo'. Both correct, both common.

label: Attached to a gerund (vedendolo, parlandogli)
description: On a gerund, the pronoun attaches: vedendo + lo → vedendolo. With 'stare + gerund' progressive, the clitic can also go before stare: 'Lo sto vedendo' or 'Sto vedendolo'.

attributes.canonical_example: Parlandogli, ho capito tutto. / Sto vedendolo.

label: Attached to a tu / noi / voi imperative (positive)
description: In affirmative tu, noi, voi imperatives, the clitic attaches to the end: dammi, dimmi, parla(mi). Note the consonant doubling on monosyllabic imperatives (da, di, fa, va, sta) when a clitic attaches: dammi, dimmelo, fammelo, vacci, stammi.

attributes.common_miss: Mi dì! / Lo dammi! (separated or wrong)

label: Consonant doubling on monosyllabic imperatives (dammi, fammelo)
description: The five monosyllabic tu imperatives (da', di', fa', va', sta') double the initial consonant of the attaching clitic: da + mi → dammi, fa + me + lo → fammelo. Exception: gli does NOT double (digli, not diggli).

attributes.canonical_example: dammi, dimmi, dimmelo, fammelo, vacci, stammi
attributes.common_miss: dami / dimmilo / dami lo (single consonant or wrong cluster)

label: Negative tu imperative: clitic before the infinitive form (non mi parlare / non parlarmi)
description: The negative tu imperative uses 'non + infinitive'. The clitic can either precede or attach: 'non mi parlare' or 'non parlarmi'. Both correct.

label: Formal Lei imperative: clitic goes BEFORE the verb
description: Unlike tu/noi/voi imperatives, the formal Lei imperative (which uses the subjunctive form) keeps the clitic in the pre-verb position: 'Mi dica!' (formal) vs 'Dimmi!' (informal).

label: Special uses (lo neutral, stressed forms, etc.)
description: Edge cases: neuter lo replacing a clause or predicate, stressed object pronouns for emphasis (a me, a te), and capitalisation conventions for formal Lei.

label: Neuter lo (replacing a clause or a predicate)
description: Lo can stand for an entire clause or a predicate, regardless of grammatical gender: 'Sai che piove?' 'Sì, lo so.' / 'Sei stanco?' 'Lo sono.' (= I am [tired]).

attributes.canonical_example: Lo so. Lo penso. Lo sono.

label: Stressed object pronouns (a me, a te, a lui, a lei)
description: For emphasis or contrast, Italian uses the stressed/disjunctive forms (which match the subject pronouns) introduced by 'a' (for IOP) or post-verbally for DOP: 'A me piace il mare' / 'Vedo lei, non lui'.

attributes.canonical_example: A me piace il caffè. Vedo lei, non lui.

label: Capitalisation convention: Lei / La / Le / Loro in formal writing
description: In formal correspondence, the pronouns referring to the addressee (Lei, La, Le, Loro and possessives Suo, Sua, Suoi, Sue) are often capitalised to mark respect. Optional in casual writing.

attributes.canonical_example: La ringrazio per la Sua gentilezza.

label: Idiomatic pronominal verbs with ne (starsene, fregarsene, etc.)
description: Verbs where ne is lexicalised into an idiomatic non-motion pattern: starsene (to keep oneself away/out of it), fregarsene (to not care), avercela con (to have it in for). Distinct from motion-andarsene because the ne isn't about movement.

attributes.canonical_example: Me ne sto a casa. Non me ne frega niente.

label: Piacere-family verbs in passato prossimo: essere + participle agrees with grammatical subject
description: Verbs like piacere, mancare, sembrare, bastare take essere in compound tenses. The participle agrees with the grammatical subject (the 'thing liked/missed/etc.'), not with the experiencer (who is the IOP). 'Le sono piaciuti i fiori' (the flowers were pleasing to her). Sits between the pronoun and passato_prossimo trees.

attributes.canonical_example: Le sono piaciuti i fiori. Mi è mancata Anna.
attributes.common_miss: Le ha piaciuto i fiori (using avere) or Le sono piaciuto (no agreement with grammatical subject)

label: Left-dislocation with PP participle agreement
description: When a fronted direct object is resumed by a clitic pronoun, and the verb is in passato prossimo, the participle agrees with the (fronted, now-DOP-resumed) object. 'La pizza, l'ho mangiata.' Distinct from pronoun_then_subject_noun: this specifically targets the agreement, separating 'missed the agreement' from 'missed the resumption itself'.

attributes.canonical_example: La pizza, l'ho mangiata. Quei libri, li ho letti.

label: farcela (to manage / cope with it)
description: Farcela is a lexicalised combination of fare + ci + la. The ci and la together mean roughly 'with it/the situation'. Used as 'ce la fa', 'non ce la faccio', 'ce l'ho fatta'. The clitic cluster is fixed; the verb conjugates normally.

attributes.canonical_example: Non ce la faccio. Ce l'ho fatta.
attributes.common_miss: Non lo faccio (omitting the ci+la cluster)

label: Left-dislocation with clitic resumption (Maria, l'ho vista)
description: Italian often fronts a topic and resumes it with a clitic: 'La pizza, l'ho mangiata.' / 'Quel libro, gliel'ho dato.' Common in spoken register; sometimes punctuated, sometimes not.

attributes.canonical_example: La pizza l'ho mangiata. / A Marco gli ho parlato.
